coredata.dc:title: Construction of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot embedded NiGa layered double hydroxide for high-performance asymmetric supercapacitors

Zhang, Gaomin

Huo, Pengwei
Wang, Xinkun
coredata.dc:description: Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are a promising positive electrode material for super-capacitors (SCs) due to multiple oxidation reduction states. However, the structure of LDH is prone to collapse during the redox reaction and has low conductivity, which further hinders the improvement of capacity performance. Here in, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD) is assembled on nickel gallium layered double hydroxide (NiGa-LDH) derived from two-dimension nickel metal organic frameworks (Ni-MOF) on nickel foam (NF) to prepare stable interlaced nano-sheets hybrid structures. As a result of the above synergies, the NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF electrode has a high specific capacitance of 2160 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and good cycling stability with a capacity retention rate of 87.5% in 5000 cycles. In addition, we also make ASC device using NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF and Carbon NSs as positive/negative electrodes. The energy density of the assembled ASC device is 78.8 Wh kg−1 at power density of 1432.7 W kg−1, while the capacitance retention after 8000 cycles reaches 81.2%. Meanwhile, individually assembled ASC device can power multiple LEDs in parallel. This work provides an effective way to construct new electrode materials with high energy storage density, good cycling performance and power density by assembling quantum dots into LDH nano-sheets derived from MOF through reasonable design.

head.ce:title: Construction of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot embedded NiGa layered double hydroxide for high-performance asymmetric supercapacitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are a promising positive electrode material for super-capacitors (SCs) due to multiple oxidation reduction states. However, the structure of LDH is prone to collapse during the redox reaction and has low conductivity, which further hinders the improvement of capacity performance. Here in, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD) is assembled on nickel gallium layered double hydroxide (NiGa-LDH) derived from two-dimension nickel metal organic frameworks (Ni-MOF) on nickel foam (NF) to prepare stable interlaced nano-sheets hybrid structures. As a result of the above synergies, the NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF electrode has a high specific capacitance of 2160 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and good cycling stability with a capacity retention rate of 87.5% in 5000 cycles. In addition, we also make ASC device using NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF and Carbon NSs as positive/negative electrodes. The energy density of the assembled ASC device is 78.8 Wh kg−1 at power density of 1432.7 W kg−1, while the capacitance retention after 8000 cycles reaches 81.2%. Meanwhile, individually assembled ASC device can power multiple LEDs in parallel. This work provides an effective way to construct new electrode materials with high energy storage density, good cycling performance and power density by assembling quantum dots into LDH nano-sheets derived from MOF through reasonable design.

The NiGa-LDH/N-GQD electrode material prepared on nickel foam has good capacity and cycling stability due to the synergistic effect of interleave NiGa-LDH nano-sheet derived from Ni-MOF and N-GQD embedded in it.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Among the available nanostructures, two-dimensional nano-sheets have attracted more and more attention in terms of super-capacitors (SCs) energy storage due to their large specific surface area and abundant exposure active sites [1–5]. Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) have become one of the two dimensional layered structures that have attracted wide attention due to its rich source, low toxicity, high theoretical capacitance, good topological composition and easy regulation of multiple cation [6–9]. LDHs can provide relatively high capacitance, but there are inherent low conductivity and poor stability due to the structural collapse of the electrode in the process of redox [10–12]. In recent years, researchers have found that combining carbon-based quantum dots with LDHs to prepare porous LDHs can solve the above problems [13–16]. This is mainly because the porous structure provides a fast transport path for electrolyte ion diffusion. Secondly, the porous structure has a large specific surface area, which provides many electrochemical active sites for the adsorption and desorption of electrolyte ions. Finally, embedding carbon-based quantum dots into LDH nano-sheets network produces a more stable structure, which can prevent the disintegration caused by the redox reaction.

The porous LDHs prepared by traditional methods due to their high charge density and strong hydrophilicity are easy to interact with molecules containing hydrophobic bonds, so the intra-molecular interaction increases their aggregation. Therefore, it is important to find a simple and quick way to synthesize porous LDHs. Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are widely used as template materials because of their characteristics such as steady structure, adjustable pore size and great specific surface area [12,17,18]. MOFs are also used as sacrificial templates and precursors of various ideal nanometer electrode materials including metal sulfide, metal phosphate and metal hydroxide [19–21]. In recent years, the porous LDHs derived from MOFs have attracted more and more attention due to its clear geometry, ultra-high surface area and adjustable pore size [22,23]. LDHs based on macro-pore MOFs can improve the ion flow efficiency between layers and effectively reduce the ion transport resistance [24–26]. For instance, Zheng et al. prepared cobalt-nickel layer double hydroxide nano-sheets based on the template of ZIF-67 with high area capacitance as high as 1979 mF cm−2 [27]. However, these reported LDHs derived MOFs exists reunion phenomenon. Therefore, in order to reduce the active site and ion transport efficiency, it is advantageous to directly grow ordered LDHs derived MOF arrays on the conductive substrate such as nickel foam (NF) [28–30].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a novel kind of zero-dimension carbon nano-materials, it not only have various properties of graphene such as fast electron transfer and theory of large surface area, and good biological compatibility such as excellent quantum constraints and edge effect [13,31,32]. These features make GQDs draw extensive attention of researchers in recent years. Some researchers suggest that the introduction of functional groups and heteroatoms can greatly change the electronic properties of GQDs, leading to its anomalous properties and applications [33–35]. Nitrogen atoms with particular bonding configuration are doped into the GQDs structure to offer required electron defects without destroying the Csp2 delocalized electronic network and affecting the conductivity [32,36,37]. Through N doping has good conductivity, so that the electron transport speed is faster [38].

Therefore, we prepare Ni-MOF nano-sheets on NF by room temperature co-precipitation method, and soak Ni-MOF/NF in N-GQD solution to introduce N-GQD into the MOF framework. Then NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF is prepared by hydrothermal method. MOF provides excellent mechanical properties, interwoven conductive network and fast electronic transmission for composite materials. N-GQD has a high electrical conductivity and enhances hydrophilicity of the electrode material because of the presence of a lot of nitrogen-doped functional groups. The interlaced nano-sheets of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF have excellent specific capacitance of 2160 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. And we find that conductivity and cycling properties of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF is improved by introduced N-GQD. The capacitance retention rate of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF is still 87.5% after 5000 cycles. The ASC device is assembled with the positive and negative electrodes of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF and Carbon NSs. The SC has an energy density of 78.8 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1432.7 W kg−1 with a very excellent the capacitance retention rate reaches 81.2% after 8000 cycles.

The agents used in the experiments were bought from Sinopharm Group, including Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 2MI (2-Methylimidazole), Ga(NO3)2·6H2O, citric acid, urea, acetone, hydrochloric acid and ethanol. Nickel foam (NF) was ultrasonic treated with acetone, hydrochloric acid, ethanol and water for 30 min minutes to wipe off impurities from the surface of NF.

0.004 mol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.032 mol 2MI were respectively dissolved in 80 mL distilled water. The 2MI solution was quickly mixed into the Ni(NO3)2·6H2O solution and stirred for 10 min. A piece of NF cut to 1.0 × 3.0 cm2 was aged in the above mixed solution for 2 h. The prepared samples were rinsed with water and ethanol for some times and warmed at 60 °C for 5.0 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD)
ce:section.ce:para: 0.42 g citric acid and 0.36 g urea were dispersed in 30 mL H2O for 2.0 h by ultrasonic. After that, 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave was poured into the mixed solution and warmed at 180 °C for 5 h. The obtained sample was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 20 min to remove large sized quantum dots. The supernatant was dried at 80 °C to obtain N-GQD. The resulting N-GQD powder was prepared into 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0 mg mL−1 solution.

The Ni-MOF/NF was immersed in 35 mL of N-GQDs aqueous solution of x (1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0 mg mL−1) concentration. Dissolved 0.127 g Ga(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.3 g urea in the above solution and mixed for 10 min to form homogeneous solution. The solution was moved into 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and warmed at 120 °C for 5 h. Water and ethanol were used to wash the resulting sample and then dried at 60 °C for 24 h. NiGa-LDH/NF was obtained without adding N-GQD. We also prepared NiGa-LDH without Ni-MOF template, and the preparation process replaced Ni-MOF with Ni(NO3)2·6H2O. The mass load of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF was about 1.5 mg cm−2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of carbon nanosheets derived from Co-MOF (carbon NSs)
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation method was the same as the Ni-MOF synthesis method. The difference was that Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was replaced by Co(NO3)2·6H2O. And carbon cloth was added to the process. The obtained Co-MOF was heated to 930 °C for 2 h under Ar atmosphere at 5.0 °C min−1. The obtained carbon nanosheets were used as negative electrode.

Field emission scanning electron microanalyzer (JSM-7500F) was used to obtain scanning electron microscope (SEM) images. XRD pattern was obtained on Shimazu-6100 powder X-ray diffractometer. JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to survey the morphology of the material. XPS diagram was obtained using the Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab 250 Xi spectrometer. The specific surface area of sample was measured by nitrogen adsorption in Quantachrome NOVA 4200e.

CHI660e electrochemical workstation was used to test Cyclic voltammetry (CV), Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in a configured three-electrode system under the voltage range of 0.0–0.5 V at scan rate of 2.0–50 mV s−1. NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-x/NF respectively was employed as the working electrode, platinum plate as the counter electrode and Hg/HgO as the reference electrode. The asymmetric super-capacitor (ASC) was assembled with NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF as anode and Carbon NSs as cathode. The electrochemical behavior of SC was tested by using two electrodes in 3.0 M KOH solution under voltage range of 0.0–1.6 V. The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) using the following formula to calculate:
(1) P = 3600E/Δt
(2) E = CV2/2 × 3.6
Where Δt (s), C (F g−1) and V (V) respectively represented discharge time, specific capacitance and voltage window of the device.

The structure design is important for all electrode materials to make the best of active materials and promote the diffusion of ions and electrons. Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1b show the preparation of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-x/NF and Carbon NSs. Ni-MOF nano-sheets were loaded on NF by co-precipitation method. These were immersed in a solution containing N-GQD using large pores of MOF to absorb. The NiGa-LDH nano-sheets were derived in situ by hydrothermal method in the surface of MOF. The preparation of Carbon NSs of negative electrode materials was mainly to use co-precipitation method to grow Co-MOF nano-sheets on the carbon cloth, and then carbonize these to get carbon nano-sheets derived from Co-MOF. In this composite material, the lamellar frame of Ni-MOF can provide effective spatial structure, large specific surface area and appropriate aperture to expand electron transport space and adsorb more quantum dots, and can grow NiGa-LDH in a large area on it. The quantum dots embedded in it can be seen as bridges and networks to transmit electrons, making the transmission process faster and more stable. The introduction of N-GQD can enhance the activity of pseudo-capacitance and improve surface wettability. The high capacitance is due to the invertible redox reaction and the proton exchange of electrochemical active group involved in N-GQD, which not only provides the pseudo-capacitance through the redox reaction but also improves the double-layer capacitance by changing the electron charge density. Therefore, the designed hybrid NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF electrode is expected to have high electrochemical performance due to the combination of pseudo-capacitance of NiGa-LDH derived from Ni-MOF and double-layer capacitance of N-GQD.

The morphology of Ni-MOF/NF, NiGa-LDH/NF and NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF were characterized by SEM and TEM. Fig. 2 a shows the Ni-MOF growing on the NF. It can be seen from the figure that MOF is composed of nano-sheets. And we prepared Ni-MOF without nickel foam as a comparison, as shown in SEM and TEM illustrations in Fig. S1. Ni-MOF is a uniformly dispersed nano-sheets of uniform size, and the particle size can be obtained from illustrations at about 150 nm. This further proves the two-dimensional nanosheet morphology of Ni-MOF. As can be seen from Fig. 2b, NiGa-LDH derived from lamellar Ni-MOF is composed of interlaced nano-sheets, which have a thickness of 26.3 nm. Fig. 2c1 and Fig. 2c1 show that NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF is composed of interlaced nano-sheets. Fig. 2c1 shows that the surface of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF becomes rough after the introduction of N-GQD. In addition, after the addition of N-GQD, the overall hierarchical structures do not change significantly. Fig. 2d shows that nitrogen-doped quantum dot with an average diameter of 4.0 nm is synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea as raw materials. Fig. 2e shows the TEM of NiGa-LDH derived Ni-MOF, which can be seen from the lamellae structure. Fig. 2f shows a clear TEM image of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF. Compared with NiGa-LDH, N-GQD is obviously introduced into NiGa-LDH network forming an excellent network structure. The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) spectra of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF further confirm that N-GQD is successfully loaded in the NiGa-LDH derived from Ni-MOF, where the distance between adjacent layers is 0.21 nm belonging to the (100) plane of graphene [36], and the lattice distance is 0.205 nm belong to the (111) plane of LDH in Fig. 2g [39]. The element mapping image of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF shows that the distribution of Ni, Ga, C and N elements is relatively uniform (Fig. 2h), which proves that the N-GQD is successfully assembled into the LDH nano-sheets network derived from MOF.

The crystalline structures of Ni-MOF/NF, NiGa-LDH/NF and NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF were characterized by XRD patterns (Fig. 3 a). Because Ni-MOF is loaded on the nickel foams, the characteristic peak is not obvious. We also prepared Ni-MOF powder for XRD test (Fig. S2). A set of diffraction peaks at 2θ = 19°, 33°, 38°, 52°, 59°, 63°, 70°, and 73° can be nicely indexed with the lattice planes of correspond to the planes of (001), (100), (101), (002), (102), (110), (111), (103), and (112) of Ni-MOF (JCPDS No:00-014-0117) [40]. FTIR analysis was performed to understand the bond cooperation between Ni and 2MI. The peak of Ni-MOF at 1490 cm−1 or 1510 cm−1 is due to the strong bending properties of the CH3 group of the imidazole-based linker (Fig. S3). In the case of aromatic substitution in the linked molecule, resolution peaks are found at 1090 and 1108 cm−1 which can infer the attachment of nickel to the N atom of the imidazoline-based linked molecule. The diffraction angles of 11.3°, 22.8°, 34.4°, 38.9°, 60.0° and 61.2° correspond to the planes of (003), (006), (012), (013), (110) and (113) of NiGa-LDH [41]. All diffraction peaks of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF are in good agreement with NiGa-LDH, which cannot be seen in the figure due to the relatively low content of N-GQD.

In order to confirm the elemental chemical state of the NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF composite, we conducted XPS measurement in Fig. 3. The investigation pattern reveals the existent of C, N, Ga and Ni elements. The C and N elements come from N-GQD. As shown in Fig. 3b, the Ni 2p spectrum shows the double major peaks of Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 belonging to binding energy of 855.74 eV and 873.3 eV and the two satellite peaks at 861.74 eV and 879.8 eV, respectively [42]. In Ga 2p, major peaks of Ga 2p3/2 and Ga 2p1/2 are found at 1117.55 eV and 1144.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 3c) [43]. In addition, the spectrum of Ga 3d has two peaks at 18.6 eV and 19.3 eV in Fig. 3d [43]. The N 1s spectrum may be fitted to the peaks of 398.9 eV, 399.9 eV and 401.1 eV corresponding to pyridine, pyrrolidine and graphite state N in Fig. 3e [32]. The high resolution XPS diagram of C 1s corresponds to the following five distinguishable peaks: 284.5 eV (C–C), 285.3 eV (C–O), 287.9 eV (C–N) and 289.3 eV (O–CO) in Fig. 3f [32]. The introduction of N-GQD into the hybrid material is beneficial to the enhancement of the electrochemical properties of the materials, mainly because the conjugated lone electron pair and electron donor properties of the P conjugate ring in the electron transfer reaction can enhance the carbon redox activity. Nitrogen replaced carbon atoms in the graphene plane improves the electrical conductivity of the sample. Therefore, the addition of N-GQD can enhance the conductivity of electrode materials and surface affinity to electrolytes and other chemical and physical properties, thus providing additional capacitance and improving the cycling stability of SC.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm was used to determine the specific surface area and pore diameter distribution of the electrode material. The surface area of Ni-MOF, NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF, NiGa-LDH derived from Ni-MOF and NiGa-LDH respectively is 283.52, 229.26, 218.48 and 63.14 m2 g−1 (Fig. S4). Obviously, comparing with pure NiGa-LDH prepared by hydrothermal method, the specific surface area of NiGa-LDH derived from Ni-MOF is significantly increased. This is mainly due to NiGa-LDH derived from Ni-MOF inheriting the large specific surface area and high porosity of Ni-MOF (total pore volume = 0.86 cm3 g−1). Moreover, we found that the pore diameter of LDH nano-sheets derived from MOF is observably smaller than LDH without template, which indirectly proves why the specific surface area is increasing. It can be found that the BET curve of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF is not closed, which may be on account of the strong interaction between the functional groups of N-GQD and N2 in the material. Due to the introduction of N-GQD, the specific surface area of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF is increased slightly. The average aperture of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF is about 2.824 nm, which facilitates rapid ion transport and charge storage as it provides a wider choice of channels for electrolyte penetration.

We know that the electrode materials were tested in the aqueous electrolyte, so on this basis to test the hydrophilicity (Fig. S5) [44]. Contact Angle (CA) tests show that NF is hydrophilic after a series of pretreatments. NiGa-LDH still has good hydrophilicity on NF, and its CA is smaller than that of NF (only 30°). After the introduction of N-GQD, the CA became even smaller (only 15.3°). This may be due to the fact that the functional groups on the surface of N-GQD make the material super-hydrophilic, which makes better contact between material and electrolyte ions and a more intense redox reaction.

In order to study electrochemical properties of the prepared Ni-MOF/NF, NiGa-LDH/NF, Ni-MOF/N-GQD-2/NF and NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-x/NF, a series of electrochemical tests were carried out by CV and GCD under conventional three electrode system using 3.0 M KOH electrolyte. The representative CV graphs of Ni-MOF/NF, NiGa-LDH/NF, Ni-MOF/N-GQD-2/NF and NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF are exhibited in Fig. 4 a with the potential window ranges from 0.0 to 0.5 V at 2.0 mV s−1. Obviously, the integrated CV area of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF is larger than other electrodes, demonstrating that the electrode has greater electrochemical properties due to the great specific surface area of the NiGa-LDH derived from Ni-MOF exposing more active sites and high conductivity synergies with N-GOD after novel structural design. Fig. S6a shows CV curves of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF with different N-GQD concentrations, mainly indicating the influence of N-GQD concentration on the overall capacitance performance. We found that with the increase of the concentration of N-GQD, the capacitance performance of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF gradually reaches the best. However, high concentration of N-GQD leads to degradation of material properties, which may be related to excessive aggregation of N-GQD demonstrated by TEM of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-5/NF (Fig. S7). Fig. 4b shows the CV curve of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF at different scanning rates under the voltage range of 0.0–0.5 V (vs Hg/HgO electrode). Strong oxidation and reduction peaks can be seen from the CV curve, which is related to the reversible faraday reaction. With the increase of scanning rate, CV curve also has analogical shape, testifying that the electrode has a superior electrochemical rate performance and reversibility.

We measured the GCD curves at disparate current densities (1.0–5.0 A g−1) to further investigate electrochemical behavior of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF electrode materials (Fig. 4c). All GCD curves exhibit approximately symmetric behavior and extremely reversible charge-discharge properties, which explained the high reversibility of redox reactions and enhanced capacitance. The specific capacitance (Cs) of different current densities i computed based on GCD curves.
(3) Cs = IΔt/mΔV
where I, Δt, m and ΔV show the current density, discharge time, the load mass of electrode active material and voltage range, respectively. It is exciting that the Cs of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF is 2160 F g−1 at the current density of 1.0 A g−1, and the Cs changes to 1740 F g−1 (5.0 A g−1). Furthermore, the electrochemical property of Ni-MOF/NF, NiGa-LDH/NF and Ni-MOF/N-GQD-2/NF were evaluated in Fig. S8 and Fig. S9. The Cs of Ni-MOF/NF, NiGa-LDH/NF and Ni-MOF/N-GQD-2/NF respectively are 472 F g−1, 928 F g−1 and 1320 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1. The excellent electrochemical performance of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF is mainly on account of the obvious characteristics of N-GQD, such as rich oxygen functional groups, outstanding hydrophilicity and luxuriant defects. These peculiarities can offer easy accessible edges, more active sites and greater effective contact area between electrode material and electrolyte. The CV and GCD of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-x/NF (x = 1.0, 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0) were shown in Fig. S10 and Fig. S11. Fig. 4d displays the capacitance retention rate of Ni-MOF/NF, NiGa-LDH/NF, Ni-MOF/N-GQD-2/NF and NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF computed based on GCD curves. Clearly, it can be seen that the capacitance retention rate of Ni-MOF/NF, NiGa-LDH/NF, Ni-MOF/N-GQD-2/NF and NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF electrode respectively is 62.07%, 65%, 72.6% and 80.5% with the current density increases from 1.0 A g−1 to 5.0 A g−1, which further demonstrates that electrode material has enhanced capability performance. As can be seen from the capacitance retention rate, the introduction of N-GQD and MOF-derived NiGa-LDH can improve the capacitance ratio performance. Fig. S12 shows the function of specific capacitance of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-x/NF as the increase of current density. The current retention rates of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-x/NF (x = 1.0, 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0) are 75.4%, 74.42%, 70.09% and 56.41%, respectively. It can be proves that by adjusting the concentration of N-GQD, the multiplier performance of electrode material may be optimized.

When the current density is 1.0 A g−1, Ni-MOF/NF, NiGa-LDH/NF, Ni-MOF/N-GQD-2/NF and NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF electrode were cycled 5000 times to assess cyclic stability. NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF remains excellent cycling stability after 5000 cycles with capacitance retention rate of 87.5%, which is distinctly superior to Ni-MOF/NF (65.7%) and NiGa-LDH/NF (70.6%) and Ni-MOF/N-GQD-2/NF (75.1%) in Fig. 4e. NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF electrode has excellent cyclic stability thanks to innovative design of material structure and synergetic contribution of NiGa-LDH derived from Ni-MOF and embedded N-GQD. The insertion of N-GQD may offer a larger contact area, effectively improve electrical conductivity and buffer the structure change in the process of the redox process. We compared NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF with Ni-MOF/NF, NiGa-LDH/NF and Ni-MOF/N-GQD-2/NF by researching Nyquist plots to farther research the conductivity of materials in Fig. 4f (frequency range = 0.01–100 kHz). All curves are consists of straight lines in low frequency and semicircles in high frequency. In addition, equivalent circuits of all electrode material including solution resistance (Rs), double layer capacitance (C), charge transfer resistance (Rct) and Warburg impedance (Wo) are also given in the illustration. The Rs and Rct values of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF, Ni-MOF/N-GQD-2/NF, Ni-MOF/NF and NiGa-LDH/NF electrodes are 0.51, 0.82; 0.67, 0.72; 0.9,1.45 and 0.74, 1.25 Ω, respectively (Table S1). The results show that charge transfer impedance between electrode surface and electrolyte and solution resistance of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF are relatively small. Meanwhile, the inclination angle of line of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF is also large, indicating that its Warburg impedance is small. The excellent conductivity is mainly due to: firstly, the layered structure of NiGa-LDH derived from Ni-MOF has a greater specific surface area and an increased ion transmission route. Secondly, uniform distribution of N-GQD in the LDH nano-sheets effectively improves the conductivity of the electrode [38,45,46].

The electrochemical property of the NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF is superior to most reported electrode materials of SCs (Table S2). First of all, NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF embedded in the small size of the N-GQD can reduce the ion transport way and transmission time due to the ion diffusion time (τ) is in direct proportion to the diffusion length L of square (τ = L2/D). The interlaced nano-sheets structure embedded with N-GQD can provide a large specific surface area, create more active sites, and make the contact area between electrode material and electrolyte larger (Fig. 5 a). Secondly, the introduction of N-GQD can improve the electrical conductivity and material surface polarity, thus improving the ion flow efficiency, reducing the ion transport resistance between layers and promoting the adsorption of electrolyte ions to enhance the redox reaction [47]. Finally, interlaced nano-sheets of NiGa-LDH derived from Ni-MOF has a larger specific surface area and greater active sites are exposed for the redox reaction [48]. The energy storage mechanism of prepared Ni-MOF/NF, NiGa-LDH/NF and NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF were further discussed according to CV curves of electrode materials at different scanning rates. The capacitive contribution (k1v) and diffusion control contribution (k2v1/2) of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF were discussed in detail. The current density (I) conforms the following relationship:
(4) I = k1v + k2v1/2
(5) I = avb
Where I and v respectively are current density and scanning rate; k1, k2, a and b on behalf of constants. Fig. 5b that the b value of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF (b = 0.64) is higher than NiGa-LDH/NF (b = 0.59) and Ni-MOF/NF (b = 0.55) indicating rapid reaction kinetics. Fig. 5c and d shows the distinct increase in the percentage contribution of capacitance from 23% (2.0 mV s−1) to 75.9% (50 mV s−1). Diffusion control contribution is predominant at low scanning speed. The contribution of diffusion control will decline as the increases of scanning rate. The high specific capacitance is ascribed to short distance ion transport and rapid electron transfer, which farther shows that surface redox reaction process of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF is mainly for electronic storage at high scanning speed.

Carbon material is an excellent strategy to develop SC cathode electrode with higher capacitance, conductivity and cycle life [49]. Co-MOF derived Carbon nano-sheets (Carbon NSs) have higher capacitance than traditional carbon-based materials, which facilitates the matching of positive and negative mass loads and the testing of two-electrode systems. In the Raman spectrum, the D band is caused by the disorder of carbon, while the G band represents the ordered part. The graphitization degree is inversely proportional to the D/G ratio (ID/IG). The ID/IG strength value of 0.87 for Carbon NSs indicates a high degree of graphitization (Fig. S13). Furthermore, the electrochemical performance of Carbon NSs electrode material as negative electrode was studied. Fig. S14b shows the GCD graphs of Carbon NSs and Cs of Carbon NSs is 298 F g−1. Employing NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF as the anode and Carbon NSs as the cathode, we assembled an asymmetric SC in 3.0 M KOH solution to estimate the actual application of materials (Fig. 6 a). The quality ratio between anode and cathode is determined by below formula:
(6) m+/m- = Ca- ΔV- /Ca+ ΔV+
Where m+, m-, Ca+, ΔV+, Ca- and ΔV- respectively are load mass, specific capacitance and voltage window of anode and cathode. After calculation, the mass ratio of the anode to cathode is 0.28. Fig. 6b shows the stable electrode voltage windows of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF and Carbon NSs respectively is 0.0–0.5 V and −1.0 - 0.0 V, indicating that electrode materials are compatible. To confirm the voltage window of ASC device, CV graphs at disparate voltage values were displayed in Fig. 6c. When the voltage exceeds 1.7 V, the electrode appears obvious polarization phenomenon, and the voltage window of ASC device can be determined to be 1.6 V. Fig. 6d shows the CV curves of ASC device under diverse scanning speeds of 10–100 m V s−1. Although the scanning speed is extended to 100 m V s−1, the shape of CV graph remains stable, which proves that ASC device have good high speed energy and interfacial dynamics. Fig. 6e shows GCD curves with voltage from 0.0 to 1.6 V. The Cs of ASC device at 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0 A g−1 are 220.2, 212.8, 201.5, 185.1 and 163.2 F g−1, respectively. The capacitance retention of ASC device maintains 74.6% as the current density increases to 5 times indicating that ASC device have good rate capability (Fig. 6f).

The cyclic performance of ASC was evaluated by 8000 charge-discharge tests under the current density of 1 A g−1. The left illustration shows GCD graphs for the first and last 5 cycles, revealing that it maintains great stability (Fig. 7 a). And the right illustration shows the SEM diagram of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF after the cycle, which indicates that the structure of material after the cycle is basically unchanged. More importantly, NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF//Carbon NSs device maintains an excellent 81.2% capacitance after 8000 charge and discharge cycles. Fig. S15 shows the XRD and XPS results of the NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF electrodes after cyclic stability tests. The peak strength of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF electrode is reduced compared with that before the cyclic test. This indicates that the crystallinity decreases after the cycle test. It is worth noting that Figs. S15b and S15f respectively show the XPS corresponding to Ni and N. It can be seen that the binding energy has changed before the cycle. After 8000 cycles, the change of the valence state of the electrode material is mainly due to the fact that a large number of electrolyte ions are adsorbed on the interface between the electrode and electrolyte. Thus the surface equilibrium potential of the electrode material will be destroyed and the surface electronic structure will be changed and leading to the decrease of the binding energy. Fig. S16 shows that the TEM of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF after 8000 cycles. It elaborates that the N-GQD is still on the LDH surface after 8000 cycles, which proves the stability of the material structure. Fig. S17 shows the EIS spectrum before and after the ASC device cycle. The real intercept is a bit of greater and a little semicircle appears at high frequency, revealing that the volume impedance of device system become bigger at the end of cycle and electron transport of electrode material slows down during the electrochemical process. At low frequencies, the slopes don't change much indicating that the charge transfer impedance between electrolyte and electrode surface does not change. Hence, NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF//Carbon NSs device have good electrochemical stability.

The power density and energy density of NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF//Carbon NSs device were shown in Fig. 7b. According to formula (1) and (2), NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF//Carbon NSs device obtains an outstanding energy density of 78.8 Wh kg−1 when the power density is 1432.7 W kg−1, and maintain an energy density of 58.79 Wh kg−1 when the power density is 7986.56 W kg−1. Compared with ASC device formerly published, including His-GQD/LDH//AC (48.89 Wh kg−1 at 800 W kg−1) [32], RGO/PPy/NiCoLDH//RGO (29.4 Wh kg−1 at 8141 W kg−1) [50], Ni0.35Co0.65-LDHs//ACDC (30.63 Wh kg−1 at 4900 W kg−1) [51], ZnO NR@Ni–Co LDH CNSAs//AC (40.4 Wh kg−1 at 1118 W kg−1) [52], (Ni,Co)Se2/NiCo-LDH//PC (39 Wh kg−1 at 1650 W kg−1) [23] and SGC//GSP-LDH (20.4 Wh kg−1 at 9000 W kg−1) [53], the NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF//Carbon NSs device has excellent energy density. The detailed comparison of the overall performance of ASC device with other work is presented in Table S3. To verify the actual performance of the device, we assembled button type hybrid SC that can light up multiple LEDs in series (Fig. 7c). In addition, the ASC device can significantly improve the energy density by increasing the voltage window, while also maintaining outstanding rate performance and endless life, primarily because of cooperative effect between the battery-type NiGa-LDH/N-GQD-2/NF electrode and the capacitive-type Carbon NSs electrode.

In summary, we design and assemble a high performance ASC device based on a new NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF composite electrode. NiGa-LDH derived Ni-MOF adopts MOF porous network structure as a scaffold with high specific surface area, conductivity and flexibility, which is used to anchor N-GQD. Its excellent performance comes from the introduction of N-GQD into layered LDH. This unique combination is not only conducive to the reaction of electrolyte ions with active materials, but also reduces the impedance and improves the cycling stability. We found that the electrical conductivity and cyclic stability of the materials are significantly improved. NiGa-LDH/N-GQD has a specific capacitance of 2160 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1 and a capacitance retention rate of 87.5% for 5000 cycles. In addition, we also produce ASC device with NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF and Carbon NSs electrodes as positive and negative electrodes, which provide the largest the energy density of 78.8 Wh kg−1 at power density of 1432.7 W kg−1. In addition, the ASC device successfully light up multiple LEDs demonstrating its practical application performance in NiGa-LDH/N-GQD/NF electrode. This work opens a new way for the design of LDH based on MOF encapsulated Nitrogen-doped quantum dot hybrid electrode, which is applied to SC energy storage fields.
